blocks.text: Re: Engagement to redesign the procurement function of {{fact:f:E-2014-001.client}}
Dear Mr. Carter:

The question you have put to us is a direct one. What {{fact:f:E-2014-001.client}} spends with its outside suppliers, whether that spend is bought as well as it could be, and what it would take to buy it better without disturbing the plants. This letter sets out the terms on which we will answer it. It follows our conversations over the winter and the spend records your finance group sent afterward. It records what we will do, who will do it, what we will need from you, and the commercial terms that govern the work between us. When you have countersigned the enclosed copy and returned it to me, this letter is the agreement between our two firms.
What the Work Covers

The engagement examines third-party spend across the business and the way that spend is bought. The work has three parts. The first is a complete and reconciled picture of what the company buys, from whom, and at what price, drawn from your own purchasing and accounts-payable records rather than from a benchmark carried in from another company. The second is an analysis of where that spend departs from what a disciplined buying process would produce: purchasing fragmented across more suppliers than a category needs, prices and terms that vary from one site to the next without a reason the records explain, and specifications set by habit. The third is a procurement operating model and a sequenced set of changes, each one costed, for closing the gap between the two. We will tell you what to change, in what order, and what each change is worth.

Certain matters sit outside this engagement and can be taken up separately if you wish. We will recommend changes to suppliers, terms, and buying practice; we will not negotiate with your vendors or place orders in your name, and the decisions on staffing and contracts remain yours. The selection or installation of purchasing systems, and any change to your tax or corporate structure, are not within our scope. Where the work meets a question we have not been engaged to answer, we will say so plainly rather than answer it by assumption.
How We Will Proceed

We begin with your own records rather than a template drawn from someone else's business. In the opening weeks we build the spend baseline, reconcile it to your general ledger, and interview the people who buy and the plant managers who live with what is bought, so that every figure we later place in front of you can be traced to one your staff already recognizes. From that base we frame the options and test each against your operating constraints before it reaches you as a recommendation. Work begins on {{fact:f:E-2014-001.start}}, and we expect the engagement to run about eight months from that point.
The Team

Brenda Rodriguez, Senior Consultant, leads the analysis and the drafting of the findings and is your principal point of contact on the substance of the work. Matthew Parrish, Research Associate, assembles and reconciles the spend data and is the person your finance staff will work with directly on the numbers. Daniel Leach, Research Associate, supports the category analysis and keeps the interview record. I review the analysis and the recommendations before they reach you, and I am accountable to you for the quality of both.
Fees and Payment

Our fee for the engagement is {{fact:f:E-2014-001.fee}}, fixed for the scope described above. We invoice monthly for the work performed in the preceding month, and each invoice is payable within thirty days of its date. Reasonable travel and out-of-pocket expenses are billed at cost and shown separately. Work beyond the scope of this letter is not begun on assumption; if you ask us for something it does not cover, we will price it and agree it with you before we start.
Term and Termination

This agreement takes effect on the date you sign below and continues until we have delivered the final report and you have settled the final invoice. Either party may end it on thirty days' written notice to the other. On termination you will pay us for the work performed and the expenses incurred through the effective date, and we will hand over our work product as it then stands, with a short written account of where the engagement had reached.
Confidentiality

We will hold the information you give us in confidence and use it only for this engagement. Access is limited to the team named above and to those in our office whose work requires it. We will not disclose your information outside the firm without your written consent, except where a court or a regulator with jurisdiction compels it, and in that event we will tell you first where we are permitted to. This obligation continues after the engagement ends.
Limitation of Liability

Our liability to you arising out of this engagement, whether in contract or in tort, will not exceed the total fees paid to us under it, except in the case of our fraud or willful misconduct. Neither party will be liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss, including lost profits, whether or not the possibility of it was made known. Our analysis rests on the accuracy and completeness of the information you provide; we will test what we reasonably can, but we do not audit it.
Governing Law

This agreement is governed by the laws of the state in which our principal office is located, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules. Before either side begins proceedings, the two of us will try to settle any dispute through discussion between senior representatives. This letter is the entire agreement between us on its subject and may be amended only in writing signed by both parties.

If these terms reflect your understanding, please countersign both copies, keep one for your file, and return the other to me. If any part of them does not, telephone me and we will settle it before you sign. We are glad to be taking on this work, and the team is ready to begin with your people at Pacheco-Casey.
Very truly yours,
To: Brenda Rodriguez, Daniel Leach
From: Matthew Parrish
Re: {{fact:f:E-2014-001.client}} procurement transformation, kickoff and the opening weeks

The letter is signed and we begin on {{fact:f:E-2014-001.start}}. The objective is plain, and worth stating in one line so we all hold the same one: find where {{fact:f:E-2014-001.client}} pays more than it should for what it buys, and leave behind a buying process that keeps the saving after we are gone. Roughly eight months, one final report, built on the client's own purchasing and accounts-payable data. Our contact is David Carter, the Director of Operations, and every request to the client goes through him rather than around him. The three of us carry the work between us, and the split is worth setting down now so nobody waits on the wrong person: I own the data, Brenda Rodriguez owns the analysis, and Daniel Leach owns the supplier and contract picture the analysis stands on.

A word on scope, since it gets tested once we are on site. We recommend on suppliers, terms, and buying practice; we do not negotiate with vendors, we do not place orders, and we do not select or install purchasing systems. If any of that is asked for in a plant, the answer is that we will scope it, not that we have started it.
Lines of Work

items: Spend baseline. I pull the purchasing and accounts-payable records and reconcile them to the general ledger, so every category total ties back to a number the client's finance group already recognizes. Where a figure will not reconcile, that gap is itself a finding and gets flagged in the first pass, not buried later.
Category analysis. Brenda Rodriguez leads the cut of spend by category and by supplier and sets the order of the deep dives. Nothing is called an opportunity until the number behind it holds.
Supplier and contract view. Daniel Leach assembles the supplier list and the existing agreements, so we can see how many suppliers carry each category and how much of the spend runs off contract.

Buying process. We document how purchasing actually happens today, site by site, from the records and the interviews, and not from the policy as someone recites it from memory. This is the part the client most often thinks it already knows, and usually the part the records contradict, so we build it from the ground up.
Client cadence. A short written status goes to David Carter on a regular interval, and it goes out even in a quiet week so the client is never left guessing where the work stands.

blocks.text: The Opening Weeks

items: I draft the data request, itemized, with each field and its definition attached, and send it to David Carter by the end of the week.
Daniel Leach begins the supplier and contract inventory from the material the client has already provided.
Brenda Rodriguez sets the category tree first, so the spend cut and the interview questions line up from the start rather than being reconciled later.
We agree the interview list, purchasing staff and plant managers both, before we ask the client for a single calendar slot.
I set up the working file and the folder structure now, so the data lands somewhere consistent and we are not renaming and re-sorting things three weeks in.

blocks.text: Two items have to be settled this week: the data request out to David Carter, and the category tree agreed among the three of us. With both in place the on-site interviews start clean, and we are not renegotiating the shape of the data once the analysis at Pacheco-Casey is already under way.
David Carter, with Daniel Leach and Brenda Rodriguez copied:

Thank you for the files that came through last week. We now have the first purchasing and accounts-payable extracts for {{fact:f:E-2014-001.client}}, and Daniel Leach has them loaded and is reconciling them against the general ledger. Nothing in the data so far changes the plan we walked through at kickoff, and the spend picture is coming together on schedule.

Two things would help us hold that schedule. First, the accounts-payable extract is missing the plant-level detail for the two southern sites, so we cannot yet see spend by location there; if your finance group can send that cut, Daniel Leach can fold it straight in. Second, the purchasing-card records would let us see the smaller, off-contract buying that the main system does not capture, and that is usually where a fair share of the leakage sits.

On interviews, Brenda Rodriguez would like time with the category owners and two or three plant purchasing leads over the next few weeks. If your assistant can hold the slots, we will send a short list of names and topics ahead of each session so nobody walks in cold. Nothing else is needed from your team at Pacheco-Casey today.
Matthew Parrish
Working Session: Spend Baseline and the First Category Findings
Minutes taken by Brenda Rodriguez. Subject: the {{fact:f:E-2014-001.client}} procurement engagement, working session held with the client at Pacheco-Casey.
Present

items: Brenda Rodriguez, Senior Consultant
Matthew Parrish, Research Associate
Daniel Leach, Research Associate
David Carter, Director of Operations, {{fact:f:E-2014-001.client}}

blocks.text: The Baseline

Matthew Parrish opened with the state of the spend baseline, which is now complete. The purchasing and accounts-payable data reconcile to the general ledger at the category level, and the plant-level detail for the two southern sites, which was missing at the start, has since come in and been folded in. He noted that a small share of spend still sits in miscellaneous ledger codes that no single category cleanly claims, and that he has left it visible as unclassified rather than force it into a category to make the picture look tidier than it is. David Carter recognized two of those codes and gave us the context to place them.
First Category Findings

I then took the group through what the baseline is beginning to show, and there are three patterns worth recording. The company buys several large categories, among them maintenance and repair supplies and inbound freight, from far more suppliers than the volume warrants, which fragments the spend and gives away the leverage a consolidated buy would carry. Prices and terms for the same items differ from one site to the next without a reason the records explain. And a meaningful share of purchasing happens off contract, in small orders placed locally, outside whatever agreements are already in place. I was clear that these are the current read of the data and not yet findings; each needs the supplier interviews and a firm number behind it before we put it to the client as something to act on.

David Carter pushed on the supplier point, and the exchange was worth keeping. He agreed the count is high but cautioned that a handful of the smaller suppliers are single-source for parts the plants cannot run without, and that consolidation cannot touch those until a qualified alternative is in place. Matthew Parrish said the same names had already surfaced in the data as exceptions, and we agreed to carry them as a separate flagged list so the recommendation does not read as a blanket cut. Daniel Leach will tag those suppliers in the working file so the exception travels with the analysis and is not lost between now and the final report.
Open Items and Next Steps

We agreed what has to happen before the next session. The category deep dives on the two largest areas need to be finished so the opportunity can be sized rather than described. The off-contract spend needs to be quantified, which depends on the purchasing-card records still to come. And David Carter asked that we not put figures in front of the category owners until they are firm, so the coming interviews stay diagnostic and do not turn into negotiations. We agreed, and I will hold the team to it.
Action Items

rows: Finish the category deep dives on the two largest spend areas and size the opportunity
Reconcile the remaining unclassified spend and close the baseline
Assemble the supplier and contract detail for the priority categories
Confirm access to the purchasing-card records and the category owners' time

blocks.text: Procurement Transformation: Status Report

Prepared for the Office of the Director of Operations, {{fact:f:E-2014-001.client}}, by Matthew Parrish on behalf of the engagement team. This report covers the work of the past period and the standing of the engagement at about its midpoint.
Where the Engagement Stands

The engagement is at roughly its midpoint and on plan. The first half was the diagnostic: building a complete, reconciled picture of what {{fact:f:E-2014-001.client}} buys and how it is bought. That picture is now in hand, and it is solid enough to build recommendations on. The second half turns from diagnosis to recommendation, working the largest categories into specific, costed changes and setting out the buying process that keeps the saving in place after the work ends. Nothing to date has moved us off the plan set at kickoff, and the shape of the opportunity is close to what we described when the letter was signed.
Completed This Period

items: The spend baseline is complete and reconciled to the general ledger, including the plant-level detail for all sites, which was the largest single piece of work in the first half.
Spend has been cut by category and by supplier, and the categories carrying the most opportunity have been identified and ranked.
The pattern of off-contract and locally placed purchasing has been mapped, using the purchasing-card records received during the period.
The way purchasing is done today, site by site, has been documented from the records and the interviews rather than from the written policy.
Interviews with the category owners and the plant purchasing leads are under way; roughly two thirds are complete.

blocks.text: What the Diagnostic Points To

The diagnostic points to three sources of avoidable cost, each of which is still being sized rather than reported as final. The company buys its largest categories from more suppliers than the volume needs, which spreads the spend thin and leaves negotiating leverage on the table. The same items are bought at different prices and on different terms from one site to the next, without a reason the records account for. And a fair share of purchasing runs off contract, in small orders placed locally, outside the agreements the company already holds. None of this is unusual where buying has grown up site by site rather than centrally. What is new is that the size of each is now measurable instead of assumed, and that measurement is what the second half of the work builds the recommendations on.
The Engagement Against Budget

The engagement is priced as a fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2014-001.fee}} for the scope agreed at the outset. Fees and expenses to date track the plan: at about the halfway point of the schedule, a little under half of the fee has been billed, and the work is within budget. No change to scope or fee is contemplated.
Upcoming Work

items: Finish the category deep dives on maintenance and repair supplies and on inbound freight, and size each opportunity as a range, which we will narrow as the supplier work confirms it.
Complete the remaining category-owner and plant interviews so no recommendation rests on a category we have not walked through with the people who buy it.
Open the supplier discussions on the priority categories, within the boundary set in the engagement letter: we frame and support, the client negotiates and decides.
Draft the target procurement operating model, including how a purchase is approved, where buying authority sits, and when a category comes back for review.
Begin sequencing the changes into an implementation roadmap, weighted so the client's purchasing team is not asked to carry every change at once.

blocks.text: Risks and Watch Items

items: A share of spend remains in miscellaneous ledger codes; Matthew Parrish is reconciling it, and until it is placed the category totals carry a small margin of error, which the report will state plainly.
Interview scheduling has slipped in places over the summer; Brenda Rodriguez is holding the priority sessions and the rest will follow.

The savings figures are preliminary and will move as the supplier work firms them up. Daniel Leach is closing the last of the supporting data, and the report will present each opportunity as a range the analysis can support, not a single number we expect to revise.

blocks.text: The next report will carry the sized opportunities by category and the first draft of the implementation roadmap. Questions on the numbers can come to Matthew Parrish directly, and questions on the analysis and the recommendations to Brenda Rodriguez. The team is grateful for the ready access the operations group at Pacheco-Casey has given us to its records and its people, which is a large part of why the diagnostic closed on time.
Working Session: Recommendations and the Implementation Roadmap

Minutes taken by Brenda Rodriguez. Subject: the {{fact:f:E-2014-001.client}} procurement engagement, working session held with the client. The engagement is in its closing stretch, and the session was given over to firming the recommendations and the plan to put them in place at Pacheco-Casey.
Present

items: Brenda Rodriguez, Senior Consultant
Matthew Parrish, Research Associate
Daniel Leach, Research Associate
David Carter, Director of Operations

blocks.text: Recommendations by Category

I took the group through the recommendations as they now stand. The two largest categories, maintenance and repair supplies and inbound freight, carry the bulk of the opportunity, and both are now sized as ranges the analysis supports rather than as single point estimates. Consolidating each from its current spread of suppliers to a short list, and moving the off-contract buying onto those agreements, accounts for most of the saving. Daniel Leach has one data check outstanding on the freight lanes, and the lower end of that range will firm up once it closes. The ranges are deliberate: they are what the evidence carries today, and we will narrow them in the final report as the last supplier detail comes in.
The Buying Process

Matthew Parrish presented the target operating model for how purchasing will work after the changes. It sets who may buy what, the thresholds above which a purchase needs a second approval, and the point at which a category comes back for review. David Carter's main concern was ownership: that the new process has a named owner inside the company who keeps it in force once we are gone, since a process without an owner drifts back to the old habits inside a year. We agreed the final report will set out the roles, though the appointments are the client's to make.
Sequencing

The group worked through the order of the changes. The categories that are ready and carry clear savings go first, in the opening ninety days, so the program shows a result early. The changes that need new agreements or a system adjustment follow, sequenced so the client's small purchasing team is not asked to do everything at once. David Carter asked that the roadmap be honest about the internal effort each phase demands and not assume more capacity than the team has. Matthew Parrish will reflect that in the sequencing, and will show the effort each phase asks of the client alongside the saving it returns, so the trade is visible rather than buried.
The Final Report

We closed on what the final report will contain and how it comes together. It carries the sized opportunity by category, the supplier consolidation plan with the single-source exceptions kept on their own list, the target operating model, and the phased roadmap. Daniel Leach's outstanding freight-lane check is the one open dependency on the numbers; the rest is drafting. I will circulate a full draft inside the team before anything goes to the client, so we have all read it end to end and the figures are checked against the working file one last time. David Carter asked for enough lead time to walk the recommendations with his own people before we present them, and we will hold room for that in the closing schedule.
Next Steps

items: Brenda Rodriguez finalizes the recommendation write-ups and the sized ranges for each priority category.
Daniel Leach closes the outstanding freight-lane data check so the freight range can be narrowed.
Matthew Parrish assembles the operating model and the sequencing into the draft implementation roadmap for the final report.
David Carter identifies the internal owners for the new buying process, for the report to reference by role.
